figures.caption: Figure 1: The correct EM objective for the autoregressive model decomposes into a token-level policy gradient loss and a tokenlevel entropy loss. See Section 3 for the details.

Figure 2: WER(%) across varying sample sizes for Gaussian Noise (left), Spanish Accent (middle), and Polish (right) domains. Compares the full token-level objective (EM-tok) against its two components (PG-tok, ENT-tok).

Figure 3: WER(%) across varying sample sizes for Gaussian Noise (left), Spanish Accent (middle), and Polish (right) domains. Compares EM-tok and the variants utilizing beam search transcriptions (EM-tok-b, PG-tok-b, and ENT-tok-b).
Figure 4: WER(%) of different TTA methods over different adaptation steps on LS-GS-10.
Figure 5: Comparison of WER and average runtime for a 1-second utterance for different methods on LS-GS-10. The dashed red line marks the WER of the unadapted source model.
Table 6: Qualitative examples illustrating baseline errors and our proposed corrections. Red indicates errors, and green denotes corrected or successfully recovered tokens.

Table 1: Comparison with EM objectives and corresponding gradients in previous works. Note that while Lseq EM and Ltok EM appear as distinct formulations, they are both correct since their respective gradients with respect to θ are both unbiased estimators for EM.
